text: Hi everyone.
Today's video is about an obese patient with a large abdominal wall incisional hernia.
She had a previous midline laparotomy.
The patient has been told to lose weight before such elective surgery could be offered to her.
Whilst waiting, she presented to the emergency department with an obstructed abdominal wall incisional hernia.
This was a large CT proven obstructed abdominal wall hernia with a main bulk on the left side of the midline.
The surgeon inserts the first Visiport blunt entry trocar into the right upper quadrant of the patient's abdomen.
The video shows a large abdomen with a prominent fat apron, highlighting the patient's body habitus relevant to the hernia repair procedure.
The video shows images of the abdominal wall after the hernia has been reduced from the inside, revealing the anatomical area where the hernia was previously located.

Upon first entry into the abdominal cavity, the camera reveals the internal view of the obstructed abdominal wall hernia, with visible tissue structures and the hernia's main bulk located on the left side of the midline.
The surgical view reveals an abdomen filled with dense adhesions, indicating extensive fibrous tissue connections between the abdominal organs and structures.
The surgeon performs a meticulous adhesiolysis, carefully separating and removing adhesions within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon releases fifteen centimeters of dead ischemic small bowel from the incarcerated incisional hernia, revealing the affected segment within the surgical field.
The surgeon observes a tight hernia defect measuring five to six centimeters in the midline of the abdominal wall.
The surgeon clears all adhesions in and around the hernia site, revealing the underlying anatomical structures and improving visibility within the surgical field.
The surgeon places the small bowel, which is attached to a long loop of mesentery, into the pelvis, ensuring proper positioning within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon makes a small suprapubic incision in the lower abdomen, preparing for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon delivers the bowel out of the abdominal cavity and performs a side-to-side resection anastomosis, joining the two ends of the bowel with sutures.
The surgeon uses an Alexis wound retractor to replace the bowel back into the abdominal cavity.
It's very important to make sure that there's no twist on the mesentery or on the bowel as it can be easily missed whilst doing laparoscopic procedures.
The abdominal cavity is inspected, revealing no visible fecal or purulent contamination.
The surgeon repairs the hernia defect using a biodesign mesh, following the completion of a small boundary section anastomosis.
Please see my video on various type of meshes that can be used intra-abdominally for laparoscopic repairs.
The surgeon uses a single straight needle stitch to parachute the mesh onto the abdominal wall defect, aligning it carefully over the hernia site.
The surgeon attempts to penetrate the thick abdominal wall with a needle but encounters difficulty due to the wall's thickness.
The surgeon lifts the biodesign mesh onto the hernia defect and anchors it to the abdominal wall using protac tuckers, securing the mesh in place.
The surgeon positions the mesh to achieve a four to five centimeter overlap with the edge of the hernia defect, ensuring adequate coverage for repair.
The superior edge of the hernia defect is inadequately covered due to a large fat adhesion obstructing the placement of the mesh.
that was subsequently removed.
The surgeon places an additional bio-design bio mesh over the superior edge of the hernia defect to achieve satisfactory coverage.
Please remember that a biological mesh lacks the strength and stiffness of a composite or a synthetic mesh.
Therefore, it's not always very easy to place the mesh against the abdominal wall.
All steps necessary should be taken to make sure that the mesh is properly placed for a satisfactory repair.
In this particular patient because of the odd shape of the abdomen and the emergency repair further challenges were faced.
The surgeon successfully places the mesh against the abdominal wall, achieving complete coverage around the hernia site.
Please see my video on the tips and tricks to perform obstructed abdominal cavity hernia repairs
This patient had a return to theatre three days after the operation.
This was because of the rise in CRP and CT showed a small bowel interloop collection which was successfully aspirated laparoscopically.
If you like my video, please hit the subscribe button.
Thank you.
